planting.note: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, rocky, limestone-derived, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican redbud requires scarification to break seed dormancy — nick or abrade the hard seed coat and soak 24 hours before planting. It grows naturally on rocky, limestone canyon walls and slopes of west Texas and Mexico.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky, limestone-derived, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican redbud is a shrubby to small-tree redbud native to the rocky limestone mountains and canyon walls of west Texas (Big Bend country) and adjacent Mexico, distinguished from eastern redbud (C. canadensis) by its smaller stature, smaller and more rounded leaves with unusually wavy, scalloped margins, and greater tolerance of dry, alkaline, rocky soils. The bright magenta-pink spring flowers appear on bare branches before the leaves, like other redbuds. Heat, drought, and alkaline soil tolerance superior to eastern redbud makes it better suited to the dry limestone terrain of central and west Texas. A fine specimen shrub-tree for the xeric, alkaline, rocky garden in zone 9a where standard redbud struggles on thin, calcareous soils.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Taiwan cherry blooms in January–February in mild climates and is the best ornamental cherry for mild-winter, zone 8–10 gardens where standard Japanese cherries bloom poorly due to insufficient winter chill.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Taiwan cherry blooms in January through February in zone 8–10, producing small, bell-shaped (campanulata = bell-shaped) flowers in a vivid, deep cerise-pink to crimson-red — among the most intensely colored of all cherry blossoms. Unlike most Japanese ornamental cherries that require cold winters for satisfactory bloom, Taiwan cherry thrives in mild winters and requires minimal winter chill. It is the ornamental cherry of choice for gardeners in the Gulf South, Pacific Southwest, and other mild-winter areas where cold-requirement Japanese cherries languish. A hummingbird magnet — the intensely colored, nectar-rich flowers attract Anna's and rufous hummingbirds throughout their bloom period. The small, dark red fruits are edible though small and tart. Blooms in late winter before most other flowering trees.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tibetan cherry is grown primarily for its extraordinary bark — not its flowers. Site it where the trunk is visible in winter and where you will brush against it to feel the satiny surface.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tibetan cherry from western China is grown almost exclusively for its extraordinary, burnished, mahogany-red to copper-brown bark that peels in horizontal strips to reveal gleaming, polished, metallic-looking new bark beneath — arguably the most ornamental bark of any tree in temperate gardening. The effect is amplified in winter sun when the bark catches raking light and glows like burnished copper. The flowers are white and relatively small, appearing with or just before the narrow, willow-like leaves in spring. The dark red fruits are small but attractive to birds. Position where winter sun will illuminate the trunk, and keep it clear of competing vegetation at the base. The bark develops its best coloring as the tree matures — young trees are less impressive. A specimen tree for its stem and bark character.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sargent cherry is one of the largest and most cold-hardy ornamental cherries and one of the few that is genuinely long-lived, persisting as a substantial tree. It also has good early fall color.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sargent cherry is a Japanese species named for Charles Sprague Sargent, director of the Arnold Arboretum, and is one of the largest and most stately of ornamental cherries, developing into a substantial, wide-spreading tree with reddish-brown bark marked by horizontal lenticels. The flowers are deep pink, single, appearing in clusters in early spring — earlier than most Japanese cherries. The fall color is early and vivid — rich orange and crimson tones appearing weeks before most other trees. More cold-hardy and longer-lived than many ornamental cherries and a parent of several widely planted cultivars including 'Accolade'. The small, dark purplish-black fruits are eaten by birds. One of the finest and most substantial ornamental cherry species for long-term landscape use.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in late winter to early spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; balsam poplar grows naturally along stream banks, lake shores, and wet floodplains in boreal forests across Canada and the northern US. Very fast growing but needs moist soil.

directions: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, deep, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun along water features, floodplains, and wet areas; balsam poplar is the most widespread tree of the boreal forest, forming groves along rivers and lake shores from Alaska to Newfoundland. Its common name 'balm of Gilead' reflects the intensely sweet, balsamic fragrance of the opening buds in spring — one of the most distinctive and beloved spring fragrances of the northern forest. The sticky, resinous buds have been used in traditional medicine as a cough remedy and wound herb, and the bud resin has been used by bees (as propolis) and by indigenous peoples as a wound plaster. A very fast-growing, large tree requiring moist to wet soil and ample space. The female trees produce cottony seed fluff that can be messy; male cuttings are often preferred for planting.
